source:
# ============================================
#  Test Connection to Azure SQL
# ============================================


# Azure SQL connection details

server   = "stocks-sql-server.database.windows.net"
database = "stocks-db"
username = "sqladmin"
password = "Azuresql@237"  

# Build the JDBC URL
# Format: jdbc:sqlserver://server:port;database=name
jdbc_url = f"jdbc:sqlserver://{server}:1433;database={database}"

# Connection properties dictionary
# Tells Spark HOW to authenticate
connection_properties = {
    "user"    : username,
    "password": password,
    "driver"  : "com.microsoft.sqlserver.jdbc.SQLServerDriver"
}

print(" Connection details configured!")
print(f"   Server  : {server}")
print(f"   Database: {database}")
print(f"   JDBC URL: {jdbc_url}")

# ============================================
# Read raw_stocks from Azure SQL
# into a PySpark DataFrame
# ============================================

print("Reading raw_stock from Azure SQL....")

df_stocks = spark.read.jdbc(
    url = jdbc_url,
    table = "raw_stocks",
    properties = connection_properties
)

# .cache() tells Spark to keep this DataFrame in memory
# so we don't re-read from Azure SQL every time we use it
df_stocks.cache()

#counting the number of rows read
row_count = df_stocks.count()
print(f" Successfully read raw_stocks!")
print(f" Total rows: {row_count}")

# printSchema() shows the structure of the DataFrame
print("\nSchema (columns and data types):")
df_stocks.printSchema()
